topic: An employee discovers the company is being sold
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias sat at his desk, the fluorescent lights humming a monotonous, irritating tune. He’d been reviewing old departmental restructuring documents, a task assigned with a sense of bureaucratic finality, when a memo landed in his inbox. It wasn't about new procedures or budget cuts; it was about an acquisition. His hands began to tremble slightly as he reread the bolded text. The silence of the office suddenly felt heavy, suffocating. He pressed his palms flat against the cool laminate of his desk, trying to anchor himself against a sudden, dizzying lurch in his stomach. Every word seemed to echo hollowly in the empty space where his certainty used to reside. He stood up abruptly, pacing the narrow confines of his cubicle, his breath catching in shallow, quick gasps. He kept glancing toward the glass partition separating him from the open-plan area, as if expecting someone to materialize and confirm the unsettling reality settling over him.

[story 2]

Sarah stared at the polished mahogany conference table. The presentation slides detailing the merger were stark and unforgiving. She kept running a hand through her hair, the strands feeling suddenly too thick, too heavy. Her jaw tightened until a dull ache bloomed beneath her ear. "This can't be happening," she murmured, the words barely audible above the distant clatter of keyboards. She picked up a pen, turning it over and over until the plastic felt slick in her grip. Her gaze drifted to the framed company logo on the wall—a symbol she had taken for granted for years—and a profound sense of disorientation washed over her. She pushed her chair back, the scrape of metal on the floor sounding deafening in the quiet room. She needed air, needed distance, needed to process the sudden, cold realization that the structure she had built her career upon was dissolving beneath her feet.

[story 3]

Mark found himself lingering by the water cooler, the lukewarm water doing nothing to soothe the tightness in his chest. He had overheard fragments of conversation—whispers in hallways, clipped exchanges in the breakroom—that painted a picture far more alarming than any official announcement. His vision seemed to narrow, focusing intensely on the chipped paint of the wall behind him. He felt a strange detachment, as if he were watching a play where he was no longer an actor, but an observer of his own obsolescence. He took a deep, ragged breath, the air scraping painfully in his lungs. When he finally walked back to his workstation, his movements were slow, deliberate, each step weighted with an unspoken dread. He didn't touch his keyboard; he simply sat, his eyes fixed on the monitor, refusing to acknowledge the unfolding narrative playing out on the screen.
